questions.question: How does the Java String pool work? When does == work for Strings?
questions.quickAnswer: The String pool is a special heap region where String literals are interned — if the same string already exists, the literal reuses the existing object. == compares references, so two literals with the same content share a reference and == returns true. new String("x") always creates a new heap object outside the pool, so == with a literal returns false.
keyPoints: String literals: "abc" — automatically interned in the pool at class loading
String.intern(): manually adds a runtime string to the pool
new String("abc"): creates a new object outside the pool — == is false
"abc" == "abc" → true (same pool reference); new String("abc") == "abc" → false
Always use .equals() for string comparison — never ==
Pool moved from PermGen to Heap in Java 7 — collected by GC when unreachable

questions.question: What are strong, soft, weak, and phantom references in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: Reference strength controls when the GC can collect an object. Strong (normal) references prevent GC. Soft references are collected only under memory pressure — good for caches. Weak references are collected eagerly at the next GC cycle — used in WeakHashMap. Phantom references are for cleanup actions before the object memory is reclaimed.
keyPoints: Strong: normal variable reference — object never collected while the reference exists
SoftReference<T>: GC collects only when memory is low — in-memory cache use case
WeakReference<T>: collected at next GC — WeakHashMap uses these for keys
PhantomReference<T>: always returns null from get(); used with ReferenceQueue for post-mortem cleanup
Java 9+ Cleaner: preferred over PhantomReference for resource cleanup

questions.question: What is the ClassLoader hierarchy in Java? What is the parent delegation model?
questions.quickAnswer: Java has three built-in classloaders: Bootstrap (loads rt.jar/core JDK), Platform/Extension (loads ext directory), and Application (loads classpath). When a class is requested, the classloader delegates to its parent first. Only if the parent can't load it does the child try. This prevents user code from overriding JDK classes.
keyPoints: Bootstrap ClassLoader: loads java.lang, java.util — written in native code, no Java parent
Platform ClassLoader (Java 9+) / Extension ClassLoader (Java 8): loads javax.*, ext/ JARs
Application ClassLoader: loads your classpath and app JARs
Parent delegation: child asks parent first — prevents java.lang.String from being replaced
Custom ClassLoader: override loadClass() or findClass() — used by frameworks (Spring, OSGi, Tomcat)

questions.question: What is Java Reflection and when would you use it?
questions.quickAnswer: Reflection allows code to inspect and manipulate classes, methods, and fields at runtime — even private ones. Frameworks like Spring (for @Autowired injection), Jackson (for JSON serialization), and JUnit (for test discovery) rely heavily on reflection. It is slower than direct calls and bypasses compile-time safety, so use it only in framework code or tooling.
keyPoints: Class.forName("com.example.MyClass"): load a class by name at runtime
clazz.getDeclaredFields(): get all fields including private
field.setAccessible(true): bypass private visibility (requires module permission in Java 9+)
method.invoke(obj, args): call a method dynamically
Performance: ~10-100x slower than direct calls; use MethodHandles for performance-sensitive code

questions.question: What is the Strategy design pattern? Give a Java example.
questions.quickAnswer: Strategy defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. The context holds a reference to a strategy interface and delegates work to it. This lets you swap algorithms (sorting, payment, discounting) at runtime without changing the context. In Java, lambdas are often used as lightweight strategies.
keyPoints: Context: holds a Strategy reference; calls strategy.execute()
Strategy interface: defines the algorithm contract
ConcreteStrategy: implements the algorithm — can be swapped at runtime
Open/Closed Principle: add new strategies without modifying the context
Java 8+: functional interfaces + lambdas replace simple one-method strategy classes

questions.question: What is the Observer design pattern? How does Java support it?
questions.quickAnswer: Observer defines a one-to-many dependency: when a Subject changes state, all registered Observers are notified. Java's EventListener mechanism, Spring's ApplicationEvent, and reactive streams are all Observer pattern variants. The subject maintains a list of observers and calls notify on them.
keyPoints: Subject/Publisher: maintains observer list; calls update() on each observer on state change
Observer/Listener: implements a callback interface (update, onEvent, etc.)
Java deprecated Observable class: use custom interfaces or Spring events instead
GUI: ActionListener on buttons is the Observer pattern
Spring: ApplicationEventPublisher + @EventListener — loose coupling via events

questions.question: What is the Decorator design pattern? How is it different from subclassing?
questions.quickAnswer: Decorator wraps an object to add behavior dynamically without subclassing. Multiple decorators can be stacked. Subclassing adds behavior statically at compile time and causes a class explosion when you have multiple independent dimensions of variation. Java I/O streams (BufferedInputStream wrapping FileInputStream) are the classic example.
keyPoints: Decorator wraps the same interface and delegates to the wrapped object, then adds behavior
Stackable: BufferedInputStream(GZIPInputStream(FileInputStream(file)))
No class explosion: 3 features × 3 types = 9 subclasses vs 3+3 decorators
Open/Closed: add new behaviors by creating new decorator classes
Java I/O: InputStream, Reader, Writer are all Decorator pattern hierarchies

questions.question: What is the Template Method design pattern?
questions.quickAnswer: Template Method defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class and lets subclasses override specific steps without changing the algorithm's structure. The base class calls abstract hook methods that each subclass implements differently.
keyPoints: Abstract base class: implements the algorithm skeleton; calls abstract hook methods
Concrete subclass: implements only the hooks — can't change the order of steps
Hollywood Principle: 'Don't call us, we'll call you' — base calls subclass, not the reverse
Examples: JUnit's TestCase (setUp/tearDown hooks), AbstractList (get/size), Spring's JdbcTemplate
Template Method uses inheritance; Strategy uses composition — prefer composition

questions.question: What is the difference between Factory Method and Abstract Factory patterns?
questions.quickAnswer: Factory Method defines a method for creating objects in a base class; subclasses override it to choose the concrete type. Abstract Factory provides an interface for creating families of related objects — you switch the entire factory to switch the product family. Abstract Factory is Factory Method applied to a whole product suite.
keyPoints: Factory Method: one product, subclass decides the type; uses inheritance
Abstract Factory: multiple related products; uses composition — client holds a factory reference
Example: Factory Method — Logger.getLogger(); Abstract Factory — UI toolkit creating Button + Checkbox + Dialog for a platform
Both decouple creation from usage
Static factory method (e.g. Optional.of(), List.of()) is a simpler variant, not a design pattern

questions.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap achieve thread safety without a global lock?
questions.quickAnswer: ConcurrentHashMap uses segment-level locking (Java 7) and CAS + synchronized on individual bucket heads (Java 8+). Reads are lock-free. Writes lock only the affected bucket. This allows multiple threads to read and write concurrently to different buckets, making it dramatically faster than Collections.synchronizedMap() under contention.
keyPoints: Java 8: bucket-level synchronized + CAS for lock-free updates
Reads: fully lock-free — uses volatile reads on bucket array
Writes: lock only the specific bucket (linked list head) being modified
computeIfAbsent(), merge(), compute(): atomic compound operations
size() is approximate; mappingCount() returns long — prefer it for large maps
Does NOT allow null keys or values (unlike HashMap)

questions.question: What is CopyOnWriteArrayList and when do you use it?
questions.quickAnswer: CopyOnWriteArrayList creates a fresh copy of the underlying array on every write operation. Reads are lock-free and iterate over a stable snapshot. Writes are expensive (O(n) copy). Use it when reads dominate and writes are rare — event listener lists, observer registries. Never use it for write-heavy concurrent lists.
keyPoints: Write (add/remove/set): copies entire array, modifies copy, swaps in atomically
Read (get/iterator): lock-free, sees the snapshot at iterator creation time
Iterators never throw ConcurrentModificationException
Memory: two copies of array during write — memory-intensive for large lists
Use case: read-heavy, rarely-modified lists — Spring's EventListenerRegistry

questions.question: What is BlockingQueue? Name its main implementations and use cases.
questions.quickAnswer: BlockingQueue is a thread-safe queue that blocks the producer when full and blocks the consumer when empty. It's the backbone of the producer-consumer pattern. ArrayBlockingQueue is bounded (fixed capacity). LinkedBlockingQueue can be bounded or unbounded. PriorityBlockingQueue orders by priority. They are used internally by ExecutorService task queues.
keyPoints: put(): blocks if full; take(): blocks if empty
offer(e, timeout): non-blocking try with timeout
ArrayBlockingQueue: bounded FIFO backed by array — low GC pressure
LinkedBlockingQueue: bounded or unbounded FIFO — higher throughput than ArrayBlockingQueue
PriorityBlockingQueue: priority-ordered, unbounded
SynchronousQueue: zero-capacity — direct handoff between producer and consumer

questions.question: What is a Semaphore? How is it different from a mutex or ReentrantLock?
questions.quickAnswer: A Semaphore controls access to a shared resource by maintaining a count of available permits. A thread acquires a permit before accessing the resource and releases it after. A mutex (or ReentrantLock) is a binary semaphore — only one thread at a time. A counting semaphore allows N threads simultaneously — useful for connection pools.
keyPoints: Semaphore(1): binary semaphore — equivalent to a mutex
Semaphore(10): allows 10 concurrent threads — connection pool throttle
acquire(): blocks until a permit is available; release(): returns a permit
tryAcquire(timeout): non-blocking attempt
ReentrantLock: reentrant (same thread can acquire multiple times); Semaphore is not reentrant by default
Use case: limit concurrent DB connections, rate limiting

questions.question: What is CountDownLatch? How is it different from CyclicBarrier?
questions.quickAnswer: CountDownLatch counts down from N to 0 — threads call await() to wait until the latch reaches 0, and other threads call countDown() to decrement it. It cannot be reset. CyclicBarrier has N parties — all parties call await() and are all released together when all arrive. It can be reset and reused for repeated phases.
keyPoints: CountDownLatch: one-time wait; one or more threads wait for N events to happen
CyclicBarrier: N threads all wait for each other at the barrier; all released together
CountDownLatch.countDown() can be called by any thread any number of times
CyclicBarrier.await() must be called by exactly N distinct threads to release
CyclicBarrier is reusable (reset between phases); CountDownLatch is single-use
Use CountDownLatch for 'wait for dependencies to start'; CyclicBarrier for 'synchronize parallel workers at phase end'

questions.question: How does try-with-resources work? What is AutoCloseable?
questions.quickAnswer: Try-with-resources (Java 7+) automatically calls close() on resources declared in the try(...) header when the block exits — regardless of whether it exits normally or via an exception. The resource class must implement AutoCloseable (which has a single close() method). Multiple resources are closed in reverse declaration order.
keyPoints: Resources declared in try(): closed in reverse order on exit
AutoCloseable.close() called even if an exception is thrown
Suppressed exceptions: if close() throws while an exception is already propagating, it's suppressed
Works with any AutoCloseable: connections, streams, locks
Multiple resources: try (A a = new A(); B b = new B()) — b.close() then a.close()

questions.question: What causes a deadlock in Java? How do you detect and prevent it?
questions.quickAnswer: A deadlock occurs when Thread A holds Lock 1 and waits for Lock 2, while Thread B holds Lock 2 and waits for Lock 1 — both wait forever. Detect with jstack or VisualVM (shows 'deadlock' in thread dump). Prevent by always acquiring locks in a consistent order, using tryLock() with timeout, or using higher-level concurrent utilities.
keyPoints: Four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Prevention: consistent lock ordering — always lock A before B everywhere
tryLock(timeout): attempt to acquire lock; give up and retry if not available
Avoid holding locks while calling external code (callbacks, network calls)
java.lang.management.ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads(): detect programmatically

questions.question: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM) and the happens-before relationship?
questions.quickAnswer: The JMM defines how threads interact through memory and what values they can read from shared variables. Happens-before is a guarantee: if action A happens-before B, then A's writes are visible to B. Key rules: entering a synchronized block happens-before acquiring the lock; a thread's actions happen-before Thread.join().
keyPoints: Happens-before: if A hb B, then writes of A are guaranteed visible to B
Monitor unlock happens-before the next monitor lock on the same monitor
volatile write happens-before volatile read of the same variable
Thread.start() happens-before the new thread's first action
Thread's last action happens-before Thread.join() returns
Without happens-before: CPU caches and reordering can cause threads to see stale values

questions.question: What is ThreadLocal? What are its use cases and memory leak risk?
questions.quickAnswer: ThreadLocal gives each thread its own isolated copy of a variable. Common uses: holding the current HTTP request, database connection, user session, or transaction context per thread. The memory leak risk: in thread pool environments, threads are reused. If you don't call remove() after use, the ThreadLocal value persists in the pooled thread indefinitely.
keyPoints: Each thread has its own value; threads don't share the variable
get(), set(value), remove() — always call remove() in a finally block in thread pools
Memory leak: thread pool thread → ThreadLocalMap holds a reference → object never collected
Spring uses ThreadLocal internally for transactional context, SecurityContextHolder
InheritableThreadLocal: child threads inherit parent's value (risk: child overwrites parent's value)

questions.question: What is the difference between Runnable and Callable?
questions.quickAnswer: Runnable has a void run() method — it cannot return a value or throw a checked exception. Callable<V> has V call() — it returns a value and can throw checked exceptions. Use Callable with ExecutorService.submit() to get a Future<V> for the result.
keyPoints: Runnable: @FunctionalInterface, void run() — no return, no checked exception
Callable<V>: @FunctionalInterface, V call() throws Exception — returns value, can throw
ExecutorService.submit(Callable): returns Future<V>
ExecutorService.submit(Runnable): returns Future<?> with null result
Future.get(): blocks until result is available; throws ExecutionException wrapping any exception

questions.question: What is the difference between Iterable and Iterator? How do you implement a custom iterator?
questions.quickAnswer: Iterable<T> is a container that can produce an Iterator. Iterator<T> is the cursor that walks through the container — it has hasNext() and next(). Implementing Iterable<T> on your collection allows it to be used in for-each loops. The iterator's remove() method is the only safe way to remove an element during iteration.
keyPoints: Iterable: has iterator() method — enables for-each (enhanced for loop)
Iterator: hasNext(), next(), optional remove() — stateful cursor
Fail-fast: ArrayList/HashMap iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException if modified outside iterator
iterator.remove(): safe removal during iteration
Custom collection: implement Iterable<T> and return an anonymous Iterator — enables for-each
